The
 
prognosis
 
in
 
high-grade
 
serous
 
cancer
 
(HGSC)
 
is
 
profoundly
 
shaped
 
by
 
the
 
spatial
 
organisation
 
of
 
the
 
tumour
 
microenvironment
 
(TME).
 
Advances
 
in
 
spatial
 
omics
 
have
 
enabled
 
the
 
application
 
of
 
the
 
ecological
 
“niche”
 
concept
 
to
 
characterise
 
TME
 
components.
 
However ,
 
the
 
relationship
 
between
 
niche
 
function
 
and
 
the
 
physical
 
architecture
 
remains
 
unclear ,
 
and
 
we
 
aim
 
to
 
elucidate
 
it.
 
We
 
profiled
 
the
 
TME
 
and
 
spatial
 
immune
 
niches
 
of
 
80
 
chemo-naïve

the
 
physical
 
architecture
 
remains
 
unclear ,
 
and
 
we
 
aim
 
to
 
elucidate
 
it.
 
We
 
profiled
 
the
 
TME
 
and
 
spatial
 
immune
 
niches
 
of
 
80
 
chemo-naïve
 
HGSC
 
patients
 
from
 
the
 
OncosysOva
 
prospective
 
clinical
 
trial
 
(NCT061 17384).
 
Integrated
 
multi-omic
 
and
 
clinical
 
datasets
 
included
 
spatial
 
proteomics
 
via
 
tissue-based
 
cyclic
 
immunofluorescence
 
(tCycIF),
 
whole-exome
 
sequencing
 
(WES),
 
bulk
 
RNA-seq,
 
homologous
 
recombination
 
deficiency
 
(HRD)
 
status
 
and
 
progression
 
outcomes.
 
 
We
 
applied
 
whole-slide
 
t-CycIF
 
imaging
 
to
 
profile
 
the
 
spatial
 
architecture
 
of
 
the
 
single-cell
 
TME,
 
segmenting
 
and
 
quantifying
 
58561942
 
cells
 
across
 
79
 
tumour
 
samples.
 
To
 
investigate
 
whether
 
spatial
 
features
 
distinguish
 
clinical
 
outcomes
 
within
 
genomic
 
contexts,
 
we
 
compared
 
single-cell
 
spatial
 
metrics
 
between
 
patients
 
with
 
or
 
without
 
progression
 
(within
 
12
 
months),
 
separately
 
in
 
HRD
 
and
 
homologous
 
recombination
 
proficiency
 
(HRP)
 
groups.
 
In
 
HRP
 
tumours,
 
non-progressed
 
samples
 
exhibited
 
greater
 
B
 
cell
 
infiltration,
 
increased
 
CD4
⁺
 
T
 
cell
 
density
 
within
 
tumour
 
regions
 
and
 
closer
 
immune
 
cell
 
proximity
 
to
 
the
 
tumour–stroma
 
interface
 
(TSI).
 
To
 
further

n,
 
increased
 
CD4
⁺
 
T
 
cell
 
density
 
within
 
tumour
 
regions
 
and
 
closer
 
immune
 
cell
 
proximity
 
to
 
the
 
tumour–stroma
 
interface
 
(TSI).
 
To
 
further
 
dissect
 
the
 
effects
 
of
 
genomic
 
copy-number
 
variation
 
(CNV)
 
on
 
spatial
 
TME,
 
we
 
identified
 
three
 
major
 
amplification
 
groups
 
in
 
WES
 
data:
 
MYC
 
(27%),
 
CCNE1
 
(19%),
 
and
 
MECOM
 
(12%).
 
CCNE1
 
amplification
 
correlated
 
with
 
extended
 
TSI
 
distances,
 
suggesting
 
a
 
link
 
between
 
genomic
 
drivers,
 
spatial
 
immune
 
organisation
 
and
 
prognosis.
 
We
 
estimated
 
cell-type
 
proportions
 
from
 
bulk
 
RNA-seq
 
using
 
Kassandra
 
and
 
tumour-cell
 
expression
 
via
 
PRISM;
 
both
 
aligned
 
with
 
t-CycIF ,

RNA-seq,
 
and
 
WES-based
 
ASCA T-purity .
 
CNV-status
 
assessed
 
differential
 
gene
 
expression
 
analysis.
 
 
Our
 
analysis
 
reveals
 
that
 
the
 
spatial
 
architecture
 
of
 
the
 
immune
 
system
 
within
 
HRP
 
tumours
 
correlates
 
with
 
clinical
 
outcome
 
and
 
may
 
reflect
 
tumour
 
copy-number
 
alterations.
 
These
 
findings
 
link
 
the
 
tumour's
 
physical
 
and
 
functional
 
features,
 
offering
 
insights
 
into
 
prognostic
 
heterogeneity
 
in
 
HGSC.
